description: This figure presents a multi-scale schematic representation illustrating the ordered precipitate evolution in duplex low-density Fe-Mn-Al-C steels (with and without 5 wt.% Ni) across annealing temperatures from 500 to 1050°C. The micro-scale view depicts the distribution of B2 intermetallic phases (blue regions) and κ-carbides within the dual-phase matrix comprising austenite (γ, red) and ferrite (α). At 500–700°C, B2 forms stringer bands replacing ferrite in Ni-containing steel, while κ-carbides precipitate as nano-sized blocks within austenite in both compositions. At 900–1050°C, B2 appears as micron-sized particles at grain boundaries and within austenite, and as nano-sized discs within ferrite. The two nano-scale insets resolve the distinct morphologies: blocky κ-carbides embedded in the γ-matrix versus spherical or disc-like nano-B2 precipitates dispersed in the α-matrix. This schematic synthesizes the temperature- and composition-dependent microstructural architecture, distinguishing between Ni-Al-type B2 in Ni-bearing samples and Fe-Al-type B2 in Ni-free samples, thereby establishing the structural foundation for understanding nanomechanical property variations.

This figure presents a comprehensive microstructural analysis of low-density Fe-Mn-Al-C steels (both Ni-containing and Ni-free grades) annealed at various temperatures (500°C to 1050°C), utilizing scanning electron microscopy (SEM) for micro-scale observations (a-d) and transmission electron microscopy (TEM) for nano-scale features (e-f). Subfigures (a) and (c) display SEM micrographs at 500°C and 700°C, respectively, revealing B2 stringer bands (formed from initial α-ferrite) embedded in a γ-austenite matrix containing nano-sized κ-carbides. Subfigure (b) highlights coarse grain-boundary B2 particles (GB-B2) at 500°C, while (d) shows the interfacial morphology between B2 stringer bands and the γ+κ matrix at 700°C. Subfigures (e) and (f) transition to nano-scale TEM bright-field images: (e) shows nano-disk B2 precipitates within the α-matrix after high-temperature annealing (900°C or 1050°C), and (f) reveals cuboid κ-carbides within the γ-matrix. The figure illustrates a clear temperature-dependent microstructural evolution—from B2 stringer bands and cuboid κ-carbides at lower temperatures (500-700°C) to a dual-phase structure with disc-like B2 in ferrite and coarse B2 in austenite at higher temperatures (≥900°C). It also evidences the distinct phase formation between Ni-containing samples (B2 stringer bands) and Ni-free samples (needle-like κ-carbides in α) at lower annealing temperatures.

Scanning electron micrograph displaying rows of nano-indentation imprints arranged in a grid pattern across different phases in Fe-Mn-Al-C lightweight steels. The triangular Berkovich indents were produced using a constant loading/unloading rate of 500 μN s⁻¹ to a maximum load of 10000 μN with a 20 s hold time, as stated in the caption and methods. The image captures indentations performed on various microstructural constituents including solution-treated γ and α phases, B2 stringer bands, micro-meter sized B2 particles, and nano-disk B2 precipitates, as well as composite regions such as γ + cuboid κ and α + needle-shape κ. The scale bar indicates 2 μm. This figure illustrates the experimental arrangement for spatially resolved hardness measurements, where arrays of 50-70 indentations were applied across regions of interest to statistically determine the nanomechanical properties of individual phases and phase combinations, supporting the investigation of how annealing temperature and Ni addition affect the hardness of κ-carbides and B2 intermetallics in these duplex steels.
